evals.notes: Baseline missed tracker update. With-skill correctly sequenced backend→frontend.
Baseline did not reference prototype or run compilation check. With-skill followed design vision closely.
Baseline did not use helper scripts. Both made good strategic choices.

analyst_observations: The skill's biggest advantage is process consistency: it always reads the tracker, sequences work correctly, verifies compilation, and updates status. Baseline runs tend to skip 1-2 of these steps.
Tracker update is the most discriminating assertion — baseline agents never updated tracker.html, while with-skill always did. This is the PM skill's core loop behavior.
The prototype reference (coupleflow-prototype.jsx) was used by with-skill in eval 2 but ignored by baseline. This alignment with design vision is a key differentiator for UX tasks.
Time efficiency: with-skill runs were faster on average (188s vs 216s) and used fewer tokens (79.9K vs 86K), suggesting the structured approach prevents wasted exploration.
Baseline eval-2 was an outlier — took 287s and 89K tokens yet missed 2 assertions. It tried to build 7 tasks in one session, producing more code but with less verification rigor.
